serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of braised chicken with rice noodles is approximately 500 g, comprising braised chicken pieces (thigh or drumstick), a generous portion of rice noodles, mushrooms, and braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo / HPB Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for 'Braised Chicken with Rice Noodles' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for braised chicken dishes and rice noodle components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken (braised, thigh, with skin) and rice noodles (cooked). The input record's per-100g values (energy 130 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 11 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 360 mg/100g reflects soy-sauce-based braising and is plausible per HPB benchmarks for similar dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Rice noodles (cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised chicken with rice noodles as a composite dish. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked rice noodles, which have a reported GI of approximately 53–61 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein (9 g/100g) and fat (5.6 g/100g) content of the braised chicken component partially attenuates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (58 × 55) / 100 = 31.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 360 mg/100g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and dark soy sauce in the braising liquid, avoid consuming excess braising sauce, and limit portion size to reduce cumulative sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles are a refined carbohydrate with a medium GI (~58); a 500 g serving provides approximately 55 g of carbohydrate, yielding a glycemic load of ~32, which is high for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller rice noodle portion, monitor total carbohydrate load per meal, and note that the protein (9 g/100g) and fat (5.6 g/100g) content of the braised chicken partially attenuates the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 360 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.6 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.04 g/100g, which are favourable factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prioritise sodium reduction by requesting less braising sauce and soy sauce, choose skinless chicken cuts to minimise saturated fat, and practise portion control to limit total sodium and caloric load per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 360 mg/100g may exacerbate RA-related tissue oedema; however, anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon) and beta-glucan-rich shiitake mushrooms are beneficial components.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, choose skinless chicken cuts to reduce saturated fat, and may request omission of chilli if sensitive to nightshade compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Chicken with Rice Noodles (黄焖鸡米饭, Huáng Mèn Jī Mǐ Fàn) is a popular Chinese-origin comfort dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual restaurants. The dish features bone-in chicken pieces — typically thigh and drumstick — slow-braised in a savoury, umami-rich sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, and dried chilli, then served over or alongside slippery, soft-textured cooked rice noodles (米粉, bee hoon or mee hoon). Dried shiitake mushrooms are a characteristic ingredient, contributing earthy depth and beta-glucan fibre. The braising process renders the chicken tender and deeply flavoured, while the rice noodles absorb the aromatic sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 130 kcal, 9 g protein, 5.6 g fat (1.6 g saturated), and 11 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 360 mg reflecting the soy-sauce-based braising liquid. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the rice noodle component. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate content and medium-high glycemic load per serving, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease primarily due to sodium. Saturated fat and trans fat are low, which are favourable cardiovascular factors. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce and oyster sauce. It is not certified halal in all preparations due to potential use of non-halal sauces.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for braised chicken with rice noodles as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition benchmarks for braised chicken dishes and rice noodle components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. The per-100g values from the input record are consistent with these benchmarks and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the rice noodle component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Potassium (175 mg/100g) is estimated from component ingredients (chicken, mushrooms, green onions) and is not directly sourced.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this composite braised chicken and rice noodle dish is not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single composite entry as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific composite dish preparation is not available in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg (175 mg/100g) is an estimate derived from component ingredients (chicken thigh, shiitake mushrooms, green onions) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data and is not directly sourced from a single composite entry. Cultural metadata in the input record (primary_cuisine field containing 'Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') appeared to be a data entry error referencing a restaurant name rather than a cuisine category; this has been corrected to 'Chinese-Singaporean'. Halal status is not confirmed as commercial preparations may use non-halal soy sauce or oyster sauce; is_halal set to false pending verification.
